unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! This unit is all about school and learning. You will talk about your lessons, your teacher, and the subjects you study, like mathematics and grammar. You will learn the words for the supplies you use every day, such as an eraser and your books. You will find out how to get ready for a contest, write a short report, and use the library well. Best of all, you will learn to talk about things that happened in the past — what you studied, where you went, and why you enjoyed it. By the end, you will be ready to cheer for the Grammar Champions in this unit's story!
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few school words each day.
Work through the Grammar file. There are seven short lessons — do one at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Grammar Champions,” and answer the questions.
Work through the four parts below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Learners collaborating with books and school supplies

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the people, places, and supplies at school, using words like teacher, library, and eraser.
Talk about your school subjects, such as mathematics and grammar.
Tell about things that happened before, using the simple past tense (studied, prepared, went).
Ask past-tense questions, like “Did you study?” and “What did you learn?”
Show who owns something, using the possessive ’s (the teacher’s pen).
Give reasons using the word because.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Prepare for a contest and write a short report about a topic.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: A Day at School
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: My name is Ishmael. I go to school every morning. First, we have a mathematics lesson. I like numbers, but grammar is my favourite subject because it helps me write well. My teacher, Mr. Ibrahim, is kind. He helps us study new words. At break time, I go to the library to read. Education is important to my family. One day, I want to graduate and become a teacher too!

readings.title: Getting Ready for the Contest
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Last week, our class had a spelling contest. My group wanted to win, so we made a good plan. We went to the library. We studied our words twice each day. We checked every letter with a friend. On the day of the contest, we felt ready. We did not win first place, but we were proud. We learned that hard work and teamwork help us do our best.

readings.title: In the Classroom
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Teacher: “Good morning, class. Today's lesson is about authors. Did you bring your books?” Sophia: “Yes, teacher! I brought my book and my eraser.” Teacher: “Very good, Sophia. What did you read at home?” Sophia: “I read a story about a clever camel. The author is very funny!”

readings.title: A Learning Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sing along:
“I studied, I studied, I studied today! I read my report and I put it away. In the library, quiet and bright, I learn something new — morning, noon, and night!”

readings.title: The Grammar Champions
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Samira sat quietly in her classroom at Kisii Central Primary School. She held her pencil in one hand and her eraser in the other. Her teacher had just said something exciting.
“Next Friday,” said Mr. Musa, “we will have a grammar contest!”
The whole class cheered.
“You will work in groups,” he explained. “You will read, write, and even perform a short lesson. Start to prepare today!”
Samira joined her group with her friends Ibrahim and Nadia. Their group name was “The Grammar Champions.”
“We need to choose a topic,” said Ibrahim.
“Let's pick something from the grammar book,” said Nadia.
“What about prepositions?” asked Samira. “We can show how to use small words like ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
“I like it,” said Ibrahim. “It is simple, but important.”
The next day, the group met in the library. They wanted to study together and write their report.
Nadia opened a book and smiled. “This author is funny,” she said. “He explains grammar with cartoons!”
Samira's eyes lit up. “That gives me an idea,” she said. “We can draw pictures too.”
Ibrahim brought a notebook full of details. “These are notes from my sister's class,” he said. “She already graduated from primary school.”
“She must be clever,” said Nadia.
“She is,” said Ibrahim. “She loves education.”
For the next few days, the group worked hard. They practised their lesson in front of the class. They wrote their report about grammar rules. They checked every spelling word twice. They even collected classroom supplies like coloured chalk, tape, and posters.
Finally, it was contest day. The classroom had bright decorations and rows of chairs for visitors.
One of the judges asked, “Did you study well?”
“Yes, we did!” said Samira.
Another judge smiled. “What did you learn in mathematics today?”
“About shapes,” said Ibrahim. “But grammar is our focus today!”
When it was their turn, the Grammar Champions stood up tall.
Ibrahim began. “This topic is from our grammar book.”
Nadia added, “We will show how to use the words ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
Samira stepped forward and pointed to the board. “The teacher's pen is ‘on’ the desk,” she said. “The bag is ‘under’ the table.”
Ibrahim drew a funny cartoon on the board. Everyone laughed and clapped.
After the show, the judges talked quietly. Then Mr. Musa stood up and announced, “The winners are… The Grammar Champions!”
The class cheered again.
“We won!” whispered Samira. “All that preparing helped us.”
Later, the teacher asked, “Where did the contest happen?”
“In our own classroom,” Nadia answered.
“And why did we win?” asked Ibrahim.
“Because we worked as a team,” said Samira. “And we gave a clear lesson with good details.”
At home, Samira told her mother the good news. “I feel so proud,” she said. “I want to learn even more grammar.”
Her mother hugged her. “That,” she said, “is the spirit of education.”
The next morning, Samira wrote in her journal:
I studied my grammar, I wrote a report,
I used my eraser to fix and to sort.
In the library, I read with my friends,
And now I have learned how good learning ends —
With joy, and with help, and a clever plan.
We're Grammar Champions. Yes, we can!

grammar.title: The Simple Past Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the simple past tense to talk about things that already happened — yesterday, last week, or a long time ago. It helps us tell stories and say what we did.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs add –ed:  study → studied,  play → played,  jump → jumped.
Some verbs are special (irregular):  go → went,  have → had,  eat → ate.
Time words help, like yesterday, last week, or two days ago.
Example: “The student studied for the grammar contest.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He goed to the park.”  ✗
Fix: “go” is a special verb, so we say went: “He went to the park.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the past tense as a time capsule! When you talk about something that happened before now, open the capsule and use the past.
grammar.practice: Write the past tense of the verb in brackets. | Yesterday, I ______ (visit) my grandmother. | They ______ (play) football last week. | We ______ (eat) rice for dinner. | She ______ (prepare) her report last night.

grammar.title: Yes/No Questions in the Past
grammar.explanation: These questions help us ask if something happened in the past. The answer is “yes” or “no.” They make our conversations more interesting.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
With most verbs, use Did + subject + the base verb:  “Did you eat?”
For how someone or something was, use Was/Were + subject:  “Was she happy?”
Use was with I, he, she, it. Use were with you, we, they.
Example: “Did you forget your eraser?”  ·  “Was the author at the lesson?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Did he went to the store?”  ✗
Fix: after did, use the base verb (no –ed): “Did he go to the store?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Did + subject + base verb (Did you eat?). Was/Were + subject (Was he happy? / Were they excited?).
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with Did, Was, or Were. | ______ you see the film last week? | ______ she at the contest? | ______ they play outside yesterday? | ______ it cold last night?

grammar.title: Wh– Questions in the Past
grammar.explanation: Wh– questions start with words like what, where, when, who, why, and how. In the past, they help us find out about things that already happened.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: Wh– word + did + subject + base verb.
Example: “What did you learn in mathematics?”
Example: “Where did the contest happen?”
Special case: when who is the subject, we do not use “did”:  “Who visited the library?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “What did you learned?”  ✗
Fix: after did, use the base verb: “What did you learn?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the word DID as a helper. When you ask a past question, “Did” is there to help you find out what happened!
grammar.practice: Complete each question with the correct Wh– word (What, When, or Where). | ______ did you eat for lunch? | ______ did the lesson start? | ______ did you go on the school trip?

grammar.title: Using ‘from’ to Show Where Something Comes From
grammar.explanation: We use the word from to show where something comes from — a book, a person, or a place. It helps us give clear information about the source of things.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: subject + verb + from + the origin.
Example: “This topic is from our grammar book.”
You can also say where a person comes from: “I come from Somalia.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “This book is our grammar book.”  ✗ (the word from is missing)
Fix: “This idea is from our grammar book.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of from as a little bridge that connects a thing to the place it came from.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with from. | This song is ______ a popular singer. | The homework is ______ our last lesson. | My friend is ______ Kenya.

grammar.title: Present Tense for Customs and Statements
grammar.explanation: We use the present tense to talk about things that happen again and again, like habits and customs, or things that are always true.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
With I, you, we, they → use the base verb:  “They play.”  ·  “Teachers give lessons every morning.”
With he, she, it → add –s or –es:  “He plays.”  ·  “She goes to school.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He play football.”  ✗
Fix: with he, she, or it, add –s: “He plays football.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Remember the little rhyme: “He, she, it — add an –s to it!”
grammar.practice: Write the correct present-tense form of the verb in brackets. | My dad ______ (cook) breakfast every day. | The students ______ (read) books in class. | She ______ (play) with her friends on weekends. | They ______ (study) grammar every morning.

grammar.title: The Possessive ’s + The Conjunction ‘because’
grammar.explanation: We use the possessive ’s to show that something belongs to someone. For example, “the teacher’s pen” means the pen belongs to the teacher. It makes it clear who owns what.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For one owner, add ’s to the name:  student → student’s,  teacher → teacher’s.
Then put it before the thing: “the student’s report,” “the teacher’s lesson.”
If the owner is plural and ends in –s, just add an apostrophe: “the students’ reports.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The teachers lesson was about authors.”  ✗ (the apostrophe is missing)
Fix: “The teacher’s lesson was about authors.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of ’s as a little ownership sign. Find the owner, and stick the little ’s on!
grammar.practice: Write the correct possessive form of the owner in brackets. | The ______ (cat) toy is missing. | The ______ (teacher) desk is tidy. | My ______ (sister) book is on the table. | The ______ (child) drawing is on the wall. | Lesson 7:  The Conjunction ‘because’ | What it means and why it matters | The word because joins two parts of a sentence. It explains the reason why something happens or why we feel a certain way. | How to form it | Take a main idea and a reason. Join them with because. | Main idea: “I like the library.”  Reason: “It is quiet.” | Together: “I like the library because it is quiet.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “I like the library it is quiet because.”  ✗ | Fix: put because between the two ideas: “I like the library because it is quiet.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Think of because as a bridge between two ideas. If it explains “why,” you can use because. | Practice 7 | Finish each sentence with your own reason, using because. | I love reading ______. | We went to the library ______. | I enjoy grammar ______.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Talk About Your School Day
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three sentences about your day at school:
“Today I studied ______.”
“My favourite subject is ______ because ______.”
“At school I use a ______.” (name a supply)
Record yourself. Check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use the word because? Did you name a subject and a supply?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise talk about your school day and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Ask and Answer About the Past
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise asking a partner or your AI tutor these questions, then answer them:
“What did you study yesterday?”
“Did you go to the library this week?”
“Where did you do your homework?”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise ask and answer about the past and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess My Subject
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Think of a school subject, but do not say it.
Give a clue: “In this subject, we work with numbers.”
The other person guesses: “Is it mathematics?” Say “Yes, it is!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise guess my subject and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Sentences About School
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
Today the teacher gave a lesson about ______.
I like the library because ______.
Yesterday I studied ______.
This is my ______. (write a sentence using the possessive ’s, like “This is Sophia's eraser.”)
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about sentences about school.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Short Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short report (3–4 sentences) about a topic you know well — maybe an animal, a game, or your town. Choose your topic, add some details, and end with one sentence about why you like it.
Then draw a small picture to go with your report.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about a short report.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Past Tense (Fill in the Blank)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the past tense of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday, I ______ (study) for the contest.
She ______ (prepare) her report last night.
We ______ (go) to the library on Monday.
They ______ (read) a book by a funny author.
Answers: 1. studied  2. prepared  3. went  4. read
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Word Match
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its meaning. Write the correct letter.
eraser
library
teacher
contest
a. a place with many books to read or borrow
b. a game or test to see who is best
c. a tool that cleans pencil marks
d. a person who helps us learn
Answers: 1. c  2. a  3. d  4. b
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Ask the Question (Wh- Questions in the Past)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the question for each answer.
______________________?  →  “I studied mathematics.”
______________________?  →  “The contest happened in our classroom.”
______________________?  →  “I went to the library because it is quiet.”
Answers: 1. What did you study?  2. Where did the contest happen?  3. Why did you go to the library? (answers may vary a little)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Because Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Join the two ideas with the word because.
I like grammar. It helps me write well.
We went to the library. We wanted to study.
Samira felt proud. Her group won the contest.
Example answer: 1. I like grammar because it helps me write well.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: School and Learning learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — Sentences About School and a recording of Speaking 1 — Talk About Your School Day. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'teacher' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who helps us learn new things. | A place where you can find and borrow books. | The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring. | To finish school or a class.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who helps us learn new things.
quizzes.explanation: My teacher helps me with my mathematics homework.

quizzes.question: What does 'author' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who writes books or stories. | The rules for how we put words together to speak and write. | The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring. | To finish school or a class.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who writes books or stories.
quizzes.explanation: The author wrote a funny book about a talking camel.

quizzes.question: What does 'library' mean?
quizzes.options: A place where you can find and borrow books. | A time when we learn something new in school. | The rules for how we put words together to speak and write. | The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A place where you can find and borrow books.
quizzes.explanation: I go to the library to read and borrow books.

quizzes.question: What does 'graduate' mean?
quizzes.options: A time when we learn something new in school. | Learning about things at school or at home. | The rules for how we put words together to speak and write. | To finish school or a class.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To finish school or a class.
quizzes.explanation: My sister will graduate from primary school next year.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Day at School': One day, I want to graduate and become a _____ too!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: One day, I want to graduate and become a teacher too!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Day at School': At break time, I go to the _____ to read.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Day at School': First, we have a _____ lesson.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: First, we have a mathematics lesson.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Day at School': I like numbers, but _____ is my favourite subject because it helps me write well.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: I like numbers, but grammar is my favourite subject because it helps me write well.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Grammar Champions': “You will read, write, and even perform a short _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “You will read, write, and even perform a short lesson.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.explanation: The text says: Education is important to my family.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A person who helps us learn new things. My teacher helps me with my mathematics homework.
A person who writes books or stories. The author wrote a funny book about a talking camel.
A place where you can find and borrow books. I go to the library to read and borrow books.
To finish school or a class. My sister will graduate from primary school next year.
The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring. In mathematics, we learn to add and subtract.
The rules for how we put words together to speak and write. We learn grammar so we can write correctly.
A time when we learn something new in school. Today's lesson is about authors.
Learning about things at school or at home. Education helps us learn something new every day.
To look at something carefully to learn about it. I study my spelling words every night.
To get ready for something. We prepare for the test by reading.
